content.text: Why GenAI Demand Is Different to Forecast

Standard web services forecast request rate and compute CPU time per request. GenAI services require a fundamentally different model because the compute cost of a request is not fixed — it varies with token count, which can range from 50 to 32,768 tokens in the same endpoint. A doubling of request rate may represent only a 20% increase in GPU load if shorter prompts predominate, or a 5× increase if a feature launch introduces a long-context use case.
Three dimensions make GenAI demand harder to forecast than conventional APIs:
Token throughput (not RPS) is the binding resource. Forecasting requests/second without tracking tokens/second misses the actual GPU utilization driver.
Token distribution shifts are sudden and correlated with product changes. A new system prompt or wider context window can double GPU load overnight with no change in RPS.
GPU provisioning lead time is 14 days for reserved cloud instances — far longer than the 1-hour window SRE has for reactive scaling. Demand forecasting is the only way to avoid saturation.

attrs.alt: GenAI demand forecasting pipeline: data sources, forecasting engine, capacity actions

content.text: Capacity planning begins with accurate measurement across three layers:
Entry-level demand signal; break down by endpoint
Input load; rises with context length
Output load; determines GPU wall-time per request

Facebook Prophet is the standard tool for GenAI demand forecasting because GenAI traffic has exactly the structure Prophet models well: strong weekly seasonality (weekday-heavy for enterprise products), strong daily seasonality (business-hours peak), and a roughly linear long-term trend with occasional step-changes from product launches.
Use 30-day forecasts for HPA replica planning, 90-day forecasts for VM reservation decisions, and 180-day forecasts for hardware budget requests. Always provision to the 80th-percentile upper bound.

# Demand forecasting with Facebook Prophet
from prophet import Prophet
import pandas as pd
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ForecastResult:
    model: str
    horizon_days: int
    current_tokens_per_s: float
    forecast_tokens_per_s: float       # mean at horizon
    forecast_upper_tokens_per_s: float # 80th-percentile upper bound
    current_capacity_tokens_per_s: float
    headroom_tokens_per_s: float
    runway_days: Optional[float]        # None if demand flat/falling

    @property
    def utilization_pct(self) -> float:
        return 100.0 * self.current_tokens_per_s / self.current_capacity_tokens_per_s

    @property
    def needs_provisioning_alert(self) -> bool:
        return self.runway_days is not None and self.runway_days < 14

def forecast_demand(
    history: pd.DataFrame,        # columns: ds (datetime), y (tokens/s)
    current_capacity: float,
    horizon_days: int = 90,
) -> ForecastResult:
    m = Prophet(
        interval_width=0.80,
        weekly_seasonality=True,
        daily_seasonality=True,
        changepoint_prior_scale=0.05,  # conservative -- GenAI demand is sticky
    )
    m.fit(history)
    future = m.make_future_dataframe(periods=horizon_days)
    forecast = m.predict(future)
    current = history['y'].tail(24).mean()  # last 24 data points
    horizon_row = forecast.iloc[-1]
    forecast_mean = float(horizon_row['yhat'])
    forecast_upper = float(horizon_row['yhat_upper'])
    daily_growth = (forecast_mean - current) / horizon_days
    if daily_growth <= 0:
        runway = None
    else:
        # Time until upper-bound forecast hits capacity
        runway = max(0.0, (current_capacity - forecast_upper) / daily_growth)
    return ForecastResult(
        model='',
        horizon_days=horizon_days,
        current_tokens_per_s=current,
        forecast_tokens_per_s=forecast_mean,
        forecast_upper_tokens_per_s=forecast_upper,
        current_capacity_tokens_per_s=current_capacity,
        headroom_tokens_per_s=current_capacity - current,
        runway_days=runway,
    )

At 30 days the P80 upper bound is typically ±20% above the mean. At 90 days it is ±35%. Always provision to the upper bound, not the mean: the cost of being over-provisioned for two weeks is far less than the cost of a saturation incident that degrades all tenants.
30-day upper bound: use to trigger a Jira provisioning request to the infra team.
90-day upper bound: use for reserved-instance budget submissions.
Step changes: model any roadmap feature that introduces a new long-context use case as a step change with an estimated token budget, not a smooth growth rate.
Runway is the number of days until forecasted demand exceeds current capacity. It is the primary operational metric for capacity planning:
Runway alerts fire before saturation, calibrated against provisioning lead times:
Open provisioning Jira; attach forecast chart
Escalate to EM; confirm infra ticket in progress
Page on-call; activate demand shaping if needed

# prometheus_rules/genai_capacity.yaml
ALERT_RULE = '''
groups:
  - name: genai_capacity
    rules:
      - alert: GenAICapacityRunwayWarning
        expr: |
          (
            genai_capacity_tokens_per_s - genai_current_tokens_per_s
          ) / genai_daily_growth_tokens_per_s < 30
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "GenAI {{ $labels.model }} capacity runway < 30 days"
      - alert: GenAICapacityRunwayCritical
        expr: |
          (
            genai_capacity_tokens_per_s - genai_current_tokens_per_s
          ) / genai_daily_growth_tokens_per_s < 14
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "GenAI {{ $labels.model }} capacity runway < GPU lead time (14d)"
'''

# Push custom capacity metrics to Prometheus Pushgateway
from prometheus_client import CollectorRegistry, Gauge, push_to_gateway

def push_capacity_metrics(result, model: str, pushgw: str = 'http://pushgateway:9091'):
    registry = CollectorRegistry()
    Gauge('genai_capacity_runway_days', 'Days until capacity saturation',
          ['model'], registry=registry).labels(model=model).set(
              result.runway_days if result.runway_days is not None else 9999
          )
    Gauge('genai_daily_growth_tokens_per_s', 'Daily token throughput growth',
          ['model'], registry=registry).labels(model=model).set(
              max(0.0, (result.forecast_tokens_per_s - result.current_tokens_per_s)
                  / result.horizon_days)
          )
    push_to_gateway(pushgw, job='genai-capacity-forecast', registry=registry)
Burst Demand Patterns Unique to GenAI
Steady-state forecasting captures trend and seasonality but misses burst demand — short-duration spikes that can exceed normal peak by 2–5×. GenAI services experience several distinct burst types:
a new feature ships to all users simultaneously. Token usage spikes within minutes, before HPA can respond. Pre-scale by 1.5× before any major release that includes a prompt or context change.

data-pipeline teams run large offline evaluation runs during low-traffic windows. A 100k-document batch at 4k tokens/doc is 400M tokens — equivalent to several days of organic traffic compressed into hours.
consumer-facing GenAI features can spike 10–50× within 24 hours of social media attention. Provision burst headroom of 2.5× base capacity for user-facing products.
gradual lengthening of system prompts as product teams add instructions. A 10% monthly increase in mean prompt length maps to a 10% GPU load increase at flat RPS. Measure mean prompt length weekly.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class TrafficPattern(Enum):
    STEADY       = 'steady'      # API-integrated products -- smooth
    BURSTY       = 'bursty'      # Chat UI -- sharp spikes after launches
    BATCH_HEAVY  = 'batch_heavy' # Offline eval pipelines -- overnight surge
    SEASONAL     = 'seasonal'    # Consumer apps -- weekend trough, Monday spike

@dataclass
class BurstCapacityConfig:
    base_replicas: int
    burst_multiplier: float       # max replicas = base * multiplier
    scale_up_seconds: int         # HPA responsiveness target
    token_budget_per_req: int     # p95 prompt+completion tokens
    pattern: TrafficPattern

    @property
    def max_replicas(self) -> int:
        return int(self.base_replicas * self.burst_multiplier)

# Example: GPT-4-class model serving a chat product
CHAT_CONFIG = BurstCapacityConfig(
    base_replicas=4,
    burst_multiplier=2.5,         # allow 2.5x burst
    scale_up_seconds=120,         # 2-min HPA response
    token_budget_per_req=2048,    # 512 prompt + 1536 completion (p95)
    pattern=TrafficPattern.BURSTY,
)

# HPA manifest excerpt
HPA_YAML = '''
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: llm-server-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: llm-server
  minReplicas: 4
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: genai_tokens_per_s_per_replica
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: "3000"   # scale when >3k tok/s per replica
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 60
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 2
          periodSeconds: 60
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 600  # 10-min cooldown
'''
The forecasting loop is automated but requires a weekly human review. Run the following each Monday before the SRE standup:
Pull current snapshots for all models via
Fetch 90-day historical time-series from Prometheus range query (1h resolution)
Run Prophet forecast for each model via
Push capacity metrics to Pushgateway for Grafana dashboard update
Post Slack report via webhook — shows utilization and runway per model
Review any runway < 30d — open or update Jira provisioning ticket if not already open
The weekly review is not optional when any model runway drops below 30 days. At that point, capacity planning becomes the SRE team's highest-priority non-incident work.
Token Budget Accounting for New Features
Every feature that modifies the prompt, context window, or completion length must file a token budget estimate before rollout. This feeds into the forecast model as a step change:
Count tokens in addition x current RPS
Measure actual p50/p95 token count in shadow mode
8k -> 32k window: 3x GPU time if users fill it
Measure completion tokens vs. non-streaming baseline
+30% completion tokens (users iterate longer with streaming)
Track conversation length distribution over 7 days
4 turns avg x 512 tokens/turn = 4x prompt overhead vs. single-shot
Count chunk tokens x chunks per query
5 chunks x 400 tokens = +2000 prompt tokens per RAG request
Model the cumulative effect of simultaneous roadmap changes. Three features each adding 20% token overhead compound to a 73% load increase (1.2³ = 1.73), not 60%.
Request rate hides token-distribution shifts. A product update that increases average token count by 50% will not appear in RPS metrics but will increase GPU load by 50%.
Always provision to the 80th-percentile upper bound. Mean forecasts are wrong half the time by definition.
Ignoring batch workloads in peak modeling.
Offline eval pipelines and nightly summarization jobs can consume 30–50% of capacity during off-peak hours and degrade real-time inference latency for concurrent requests.
GenAI traffic often follows an S-curve: fast initial adoption, plateau at user-base saturation, then another step change from the next feature. Re-fit the Prophet model after each product launch.

Token-level monitoring at the model serving layer is required. Application-level RPS metrics will not surface gradual prompt-length increases that accumulate to a 2× capacity increase over six months.

Demand forecasting for GenAI services requires measuring token throughput, not just request rate, because GPU utilization is proportional to tokens processed, not requests served. Time-series models (Prophet) decompose weekly and daily seasonality to produce 90-day forecasts; provisioning decisions use the 80th-percentile upper bound to account for forecast uncertainty. Runway alerts fire at 30 days (open Jira ticket) and 14 days (escalate to management) because GPU lead time is 14 days. Weekly capacity reviews, token budget estimates for every roadmap feature, and burst headroom of 2.5× base capacity are the three operational practices that prevent saturation incidents from outrunning infrastructure.
